title: Ensure that Only Approved Extensions Are Installed

input_row.title: Ensure that Only Approved Extensions Are Installed
input_row.description: For added security, only install organization-approved extensions on VMs.
input_row.rationale: Azure virtual machine extensions are small applications that provide post-deployment configuration and automation tasks on Azure virtual machines. These extensions run with administrative privileges and could potentially access anything on a virtual machine. The Azure Portal and community provide several such extensions. Each organization should carefully evaluate these extensions and ensure that only those that are approved for use are actually implemented.
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. Go to Virtual machines. 2. For each virtual machine, click on the server name to select it. 3. In the new column menu, under Settings Click on Extensions + applications. 4. Ensure that all the listed extensions are approved by your organization for use. Audit from Azure CLI Use the below command to list the extensions attached to a VM, and ensure the listed extensions are approved for use. az vm extension list --vm-name --resource-group -- query [*].name Audit from PowerShell Get a list of VMs. Get-AzVM For each VM run the following command. Get-AzVMExtension -ResourceGroupName -VMName Review each Name, ExtensionType, and ProvisioningState to make sure no unauthorized extensions are installed on any virtual machines. Audit from Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: c0e996f8-39cf-4af9-9f45-83fbde810432 - Name: 'Only approved VM extensions should be installed'
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal 1. Go to Virtual machines. 2. For each virtual machine, go to Settings. 3. Click on Extensions + applications. 4. If there are unapproved extensions, uninstall them. Remediate from Azure CLI From the audit command identify the unapproved extensions, and use the below CLI command to remove an unapproved extension attached to VM. az vm extension delete --resource-group --vm-name --name Remediate from PowerShell For each VM and each insecure extension from the Audit Procedure run the following command. Remove-AzVMExtension -ResourceGroupName -Name -VMName
input_row.impact: Functionality by unsupported extensions will be disabled.
input_row.default_value: By default, no extensions are added to the virtual machines.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. **Access the AWS Management Console:**
   - Open the AWS Management Console and log in with an account that has permissions to view EC2 details.

2. **Navigate to the EC2 Dashboard:**
   - Select "EC2" from the Services menu.

3. **Select Instances:**
   - In the left navigation pane, click on "Instances" to view the list of EC2 instances.

4. **Review Instance Details:**
   - For each instance, click on the instance ID to view its details.

5. **Check for Installed Extensions:**
   - In the instance details page, locate the "Description" tab.
   - Note that AWS EC2 does not have a direct equivalent to Azure VM extensions. Instead, check for any installed software or agents that may align with the equivalent concept of extensions (e.g., AWS Systems Manager Agents, third-party agents).

6. **Verify Compliance with Organizational Policies:**
   - Compare the installed agents or applications against your organization's approved list of software/extensions for EC2 instances. 

7. **Expected Secure Configuration:**
   - Document any discrepancies where unapproved agents or software are installed on the instances.

8. **Confirm Compliance:**
   - Ensure that all instances either have no agents or software that are not on the approved list, or document steps for remediation for instances that need to remove unapproved software. 

This manual review process is vital because it involves checking the configuration and approval status of software that may not be straightforwardly queryable through AWS APIs.
